title: Latin: The Medieval Superstar
text: Latin overwhelmingly dominates early manuscript production, with a very high probability of use in both religious and non-religious texts during the 1100s and 1200s. However, its dominance gradually declines over time, especially after the 1400s, reflecting the increasing use of vernacular languages.

title: Ancient Greek: Rare but Persistent
text: The following graphs illustrate this shift: Middle English and English rise steadily from the thirteenth century onward, particularly in non-religious manuscripts, suggesting the growing importance of vernacular writing for practical, literary, and administrative texts.

title: Middle English on the Rise
text: Italian also increases notably in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, though at lower levels than English.

title: Italian Finds Its Place
text: In contrast, Ancient Greek remains relatively rare throughout the period, appearing slightly more frequently in religious contexts toward the end of the fifteenth century.

title: Anglo-Norman in Medieval England
text: Anglo-Norman shows its strongest presence in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, reflecting its importance in England after the Norman Conquest. Its use declines in later centuries as English gradually becomes more prominent in manuscript production.

title: Middle French: A Quiet Presence
text: Middle French appears regularly in the manuscripts across the period shown in the graph. Although it is less common than Latin, it is clearly present in the collection and reflects the growing use of French as a written vernacular. The pattern in the graph may also reflect the particular manuscripts preserved in the library, rather than the full range of texts produced in the medieval period.

title: English Steps into the Spotlight
text: Middle English begins to appear more frequently from the thirteenth century onward. Its growing presence reflects the increasing use of vernacular language in manuscripts, particularly for practical, literary, and everyday texts.

title: Old French: Early Vernacular Writing
text: Old French also appears most frequently in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries. After this early peak, its presence declines, while English and other vernacular languages become increasingly common in manuscripts.
